segments.s406-g65: به سر بر نهد = a poetic clause meaning 'to place on the head'. به + سر functions together as a locative 'upon the head'; بر نهد is the classical third‑person form of نهاد (to place) used in poetry with a potential/performative sense (here rhetorical: 'will he put...'). تاج شاهنشهی is a genitival phrase 'the crown of kingship/empire' (تاج modified by شاهنشهی).
segments.s406-g66: برو پای دارد = 'has a (báru‑/buru‑)foot/footing'. پای دارد is a concrete predicate 'has a foot/footing/base' (دارد present 'has'). برو here is an older bound modifier (broadly 'firm, steady') that qualifies پای; بهی و مهی are two laudatory adjectives ('behi' = good/excellent; 'mahi' = moonlike/beautiful) joined by و. The whole is a rhetorical question about whether his basis/support is excellent and radiant.
segments.s406-g67: چو + بشنید = 'when he heard' (چو = when; بشنید is the past tense/past‑narrative 'he heard' in classical/poetic Persian). دانای ایران is a title 'the sage/wise man of Iran' (adjective noun order: دانای modifying ایران or vice versa as an epithet). سخن is the direct object 'the speech/words'.
segments.s406-g68: نگه کرد = literally 'made/looked (a gaze)' where نگه (gaze/look) plus the light verb کرد => 'he kept/fixed his gaze'. آن زیجهای کهن = 'those (آن) ancient (کهن) ziǰs/astronomical tables (زیجهای, plural)'; the adjective کهن follows the noun in the typical Persian order in poetry.
segments.s406-g69: ز دانش = 'from/because of knowledge' (ز = from/because of; دانش = knowledge). بروها here functions as the affected body‑part 'the brows' in the poetic image (an older/contracted form of 'ابروها'); پر از تاب کرد is a causative/light‑verb construction: پر از تاب = 'full of radiance/brightness' and کرد = 'he made/filled'. Overall: 'He made the brows full of radiance through knowledge.'
segments.s406-g70: ز تیمار = 'from/through care/anxiety' (ز = from; تیمار = care). مژگان (eyelashes) is the patient; پر از آب کرد = 'he made (them) full of water' i.e. filled the eyelashes with water (a conventional image for tears). The verb کرد again is the light verb 'made/caused to be full'.
segments.s406-g71: همی گفت = a durative/declarative phrase 'he kept saying' (همی = continuously/still; گفت here functions as 'said' in narrative). بد روز و بد اخترم = an exclamation 'an unlucky/bad day and my unlucky star' (بد modifies روز and اختر; اخترم is 'my star' with the possessive suffix -م).
segments.s406-g72: ببارید = past tense 'it rained' or 'it fell' (used transitively/figuratively here). آتش = 'fire' (the thing that fell); همی repeats the continuous/ongoing sense 'continually/again and again'; بر سرم = a locative phrase 'upon my head' (بر = upon; سرم = my head with the possessive suffix). The line means 'fire kept raining upon my head.'
segments.s406-g73: مرا (م + را) is the object pronoun 'me' (را is the object marker) placed before the verb for emphasis; کاشکی is an optative/exclamatory particle meaning 'would that / I wish' that introduces the wished-for circumstance; پیش فرخ زریر is a prepositional phrase 'before/in the presence of Farrokh Zarrir' (فرخ زریر is a personal name formed of an adjective + name). The verb that takes this object appears in the next segment, so the object and locative are fronted for poetic effect.
segments.s406-g74: زمانه here functions as the subject meaning 'fate/fortune' rather than merely 'age'; فگندی is an archaic past form of فکندن ('to throw') — 3rd-person past — so the clause means 'fate threw'; به چنگال شیر is the prepositional complement 'into / to the claws of the lion' (به + noun phrase). The object 'مرا' from the previous segment is what was thrown.
segments.s406-g75: وگر introduces a conditional 'if' (often counterfactual); خود is emphatic 'himself' (reinforcing the agent); نکشتی here is an archaic/prefixed past form of کشتن meaning 'killed' (in epic Persian the initial ن sometimes appears in past forms and is not the modern negative); پدر مر = 'my father' (مر = 'me' / 'of me' i.e. 'father of me'), and مرا is the object pronoun 'me' repeated/emphasized. Word order is inverted for emphasis: the object and emphatic elements appear before or next to the verb.
segments.s406-g76: نگشتی is a verbal form related to گشتن (here functioning in the sense of 'became/turned' or an archaic past/auxiliary sense in context — the line uses an older verbal pattern that links to the following phrase); به جاماسپ is a dative/benefactive 'to Jamasp' (Jamasp is a proper name); بداخترا is a final adjective 'ill-fated' describing the situation or the speaker. The clause says that, had the father not done the deed, Jamasp would not have been the recipient/keeper of the speaker (hence the 'ill-fated' note).
segments.s406-g77: ورا is the object pronoun 'him/that one' (here 'him'); هم is an intensifier meaning 'even' or 'also' (here 'even'); ندیدی is 2nd-person singular past 'you did not see'; به خاک اندرون is a locative phrase 'in/into the inner dust / inner quarters' (به + خاک + اندرون), placing where he lay. The verb precedes or follows objects and locatives in flexible poetic order.
segments.s406-g78: بران سان = 'there like that / in that manner' (بران = 'there/on that', سان = 'like/as'); فگنده is a past participial/archaic past form of 'فکندن' meaning 'thrown' or 'flung' (descriptive participle); پیش پر ز خون is a compact image: پیش here 'before/in front' or part of the simile, پر z خون means 'a feather/full of blood' — پر here functions as 'full (of)' rather than literally 'feather' in this collocation, so the phrase pictures the body tossed there like something soaked in blood. The line uses condensed epic metaphors and inverted order.
segments.s406-g79: چو introduces a simile 'like/as'; اسفندیاری is the proper name Esfandiyār; که begins a relative clause 'who/which'; از چنگ اوی = 'from his/claw of him' where از marks source and اوی is the archaic possessive 'his'. The construction sets up a comparison to Esfandiyār's violent act.
segments.s406-g80: بدرد دل شیر is a compact verbal phrase meaning 'tore/rent the lion's heart' — بدرد here carries the sense of 'to cause pain / to rend' and combines with دل 'heart' as its object; ز آهنگ اوی 'because of/by his آهنگ' uses ز to mark cause or instrument and آهنگ in epic diction means 'intent / attack / purpose' (not 'melody'), with اوی again 'his'. So the clause depicts Esfandiyār wrenching the lion's heart by virtue of his intent/assault.
segments.s406-g113: دل شاه is a possessive noun phrase 'the heart of the king' with the usual ezāfe omitted in this poetic style. زان در is an adverbial-compositional phrase (زان = 'from that', در = 'in') placed before the predicate to mark cause or circumstance. پراندیشه is a compound adjective (پر- 'full of' + اندیشه 'thought') meaning 'pensive/filled with thought', and شد is the past/complete ('became') marking the change of state.
segments.s406-g114: سرش را combines سر 'head' with the enclitic ـش 'his' (سرش = 'his head'), followed by the object/topic marker را. غم و درد is a coordinated pair 'sorrow and pain'; هم here means 'also/likewise' and precedes پیشه شد, where پیشه 'occupation/lot/companion' is the noun and شد 'became' is the inchoative verb — together meaning 'sorrow and pain likewise became (his) practice/companions.'
segments.s406-g115: بد اندیشه is a tight compound meaning 'evil/ill thought(s)' (adjective + noun); classical Persian allows this adjective-before-noun order in fixed compounds. گردش روزگار is a genitive-like juxtaposition ('the turning/cycle of time/fate') where گردش ('turning, circuit') is qualified by روزگار ('time/fate'). Together the phrase names the things that act on the king.
segments.s406-g116: همی is an archaic adverb meaning 'always/still' marking habitual or continual action. بر بدی is a prepositional phrase ('upon/for evil') specifying the domain in which they instruct; بودش is بود (past 'was') with enclitic ـش 'to him' ('was to him'), and آموزگار is predicate nominal 'teacher'. The order بودش آموزگار literally reads 'was to him a teacher', i.e. 'served as his teacher.'
segments.s406-g81: This clause uses the preposition ز (z) meaning 'from/of' followed by دشمن جهان ('the world's enemies') as the object. سربسر پاک کرد is a compound verbal phrase: سربسر (an adverb) intensifies پاک کرد ('cleansed, purged'), with کرد the past tense of کردن 'to do/make' used here as a light verb meaning 'made clean' or 'cleared'. Word order is object/adverb before the verb (typical in classical Persian: modifiers can precede the verb).
segments.s406-g82: به رزم functions as a prepositional phrase 'in/with regard to battle' (به + noun); اندرون 'within' locates the domain, and نیستش is the present/past-negative 'is not / does not exist for him' with the clitic -ش referring back to the hero. هم‌نبرد here means 'an equal/opponent in battle' — the negative construction نیستش هم‌نبرد = 'there is no equal to him'. The clause therefore states there is no rival to him in combat.
segments.s406-g83: جهان is the patient/affected domain 'the world' and از بداندیش ('of/from ill‑wishers') marks what was removed; بی‑بیم 'fearless' is a predicate adjective formed with the prefix بی‑. کرد is the past tense light verb 'made/caused'. The sense: he caused the world to be free from malicious people (i.e., removed fear of them).
segments.s406-g84: تن اژدها را is a definite direct object (را marking) 'the body of the dragon'; به دو نیم (literally 'into two halves') gives the result; کرد is again the past light verb 'did/caused' — 'he caused the dragon's body to be in two halves' = 'he split the dragon in two.' Word order (object + result phrase + کرد) is normal for classical Persian narrative.
segments.s406-g85: ازاین پس ('from this point onward' / 'henceforth') is a temporal adverbial. غم او ('his sorrow') is the theme; بباید is a modal auxiliary 'must/ought' and is followed by کشید, the past form of کشیدن. Here کشید is used in the sense 'to bear/suffer' (to endure), so the clause expresses obligation: 'from now on his sorrow must be borne.' Note: classical Persian often uses بباید + past infinitival form to express necessity.
segments.s406-g86: بسی شور و تلخی is a coordinated noun phrase 'much tumult/passion and bitterness'; بباید (again 'must') governs چشید, the past stem of چشیدن 'to taste/experience', used here figuratively 'to endure/experience'. The overall clause: 'one must experience much turmoil and bitterness.' Note that بسی here functions as an intensifier 'much' (classical usage).
segments.s406-g87: بدو is the dative clitic phrase 'to him'; گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (the narrative past). شاه is the nominative subject 'the king' placed after the verb (permissible word order in epic/classical Persian), and ای پسندیده مرد is a vocative 'O chosen/esteemed man' addressing the addressee. The line therefore reports the king addressing someone directly.
segments.s406-g88: سخن گوی is an imperative sequence: سخن 'word/speech' + گوی (here the imperative/verb stem 'say/speak') = 'speak' or 'utter your speech.' وز (a connective here) + راه دانش 'the path of knowledge' forms the prepositional phrase; مگرد is a negative imperative 'do not turn/stray' (literally 'do not become/turn aside'). So the commanded content is 'Speak — and do not leave the path of knowledge.' Note that several forms (گوی, مگرد) are imperative/classical usages.
segments.s406-g57: همی is an iterative/adverbial particle meaning 'continually' or 'kept on'. در دل اندیشه is a prepositional phrase 'in (his) heart/thought' with دل here as the bodily/psychological container. بفزایدش is a 3rd‑person singular present/imperfective classical form with the enclitic object -ش = 'it/him', so the clause reads 'he kept increasing it/kept adding that thought in his heart.'
segments.s406-g58: همی again marks continued or repeated action. تاج و تخت is a coordinated noun phrase 'crown and throne' (two symbols of sovereignty). آرزو آیدش uses آید (3rd‑singular present) with the enclitic -ش = 'comes upon him/he feels', so literally 'a desire comes to him' = 'he is seized by desire for...'.
segments.s406-g59: بخواند here is a classical past/narrative 3rd‑person form meaning 'he summoned/called'. آن زمان is a temporal phrase 'at that time'. The proper name جاماسپ is the object and is marked by را, the definite/object marker; word order places the verb first (narrative style) and the object after.
segments.s406-g60: همان is a demonstrative 'those same' or 'the same'. فال گویان is a compound: فال (fortune/act of divination) plus the active participle گویان 'those who speak/tell', giving 'diviners/fortunetellers'. لهراسپ را names their association ('of Lohrasp') with را again marking the whole phrase as definite/object.
segments.s406-g61: برفتند is past‑tense plural 'they went/they departed'. با زیجها is an instrumental/associative phrase 'with the zījes/astronomical tables' (زیجها is the plural). برکنار is an adverb/adpositional phrase meaning 'aside' or 'to one side', describing where they went.
segments.s406-g62: بپرسید is a past 3rd‑person form 'asked'. شاه is the subject 'the king'. از is the preposition 'about/concerning'. گو here functions as a short attributive/epithet (classical usage) attached to the name to indicate 'the said/so‑called' or simply as part of the reference; اسفندیار is the proper name. Together: 'the king asked about (that) Esfandiyar.'
segments.s406-g63: که introduces the indirect/question clause here ('whether/would that...'). او را places the person with the topical/object marker را (classical use marking the affected person). بود is the past form of 'to be' used here in a modal/hypothetical sense ('would be' / 'would have'), and زندگانی دراز means 'a long life' (زندگانی = life, دراز = long). The whole is an interrogative about whether he will have a long life.
segments.s406-g64: نشیند is a classical subjunctive/optative form 'would sit / would dwell / would remain' used idiomatically for 'to be settled' or 'to live'. به introduces the states in which he might sit: شادی 'joy', آرام 'calm/tranquillity', and ناز 'pampering/comfort' — three coordinated nouns describing a pleasant, easy life.
segments.s406-g89: هلا is an exclamatory imperative particle meaning “come on” or “hurry up.” زود is an adverb “quickly/soon” placed before the imperative بشتاب (imperative of شتابیدن, “hasten”). و simply coordinates clauses (“and”). با من بگوی is a prepositional phrase (با + object من) followed by the imperative verb (بگوی) meaning “speak/tell (to) me”; note the object phrase precedes the verb here, typical of Persian clause order.
segments.s406-g90: کزین = از + این, “from/because of this,” placed before پرسشم (پرسش + enclitic -م, “my question”). تلخی is the nominalized quality “bitterness” (emotionally bitter) which functions as the theme that آمد (past “came”) onto/unto the face: آمد به + روی = “came upon the face.” The clause literally: “From this my-question, bitterness came to (the) face.”
segments.s406-g91: گر is the conditional particle “if.” او is the subject “he.” چون = “like/as” introduces a comparison, followed by the proper name زریر and the title سپهبد (spahbed, a high military rank). بود is the past tense/copula “was,” used here in a counterfactual sense (“were”). The whole clause = “If he were, like Zarir, a spahbed…”
segments.s406-g92: مرا زیستن = ‘to me, living’ (مرا is the experiencer/affected pronoun placed before the infinitive زیستن, a classical/poetic structure meaning ‘for me to live’). زین سپس (زین = ‘this/of this,’ سپس = ‘after/then’) = ‘after this.’ بد بود is the predicate ‘was bad’ (بود in past used to express the evaluative/conditional sense: ‘would be bad’). Altogether: ‘For me, living after this would be bad.’
segments.s406-g93: ورا here functions locatively/introductory (“there/for that”), followed by در جهان “in the world.” هوش = “intellect/wit,” بر دست = “at hand / available” (بر = on/upon, دست = hand/power). کیست is the copular-question (“who is”). The clause asks rhetorically who in the world has timely intelligence available.
segments.s406-g94: کزان = از + آن, “from/for which.” درد ما را = “our pain” with را marking the definite/affected noun as object. بباید is the impersonal/modal “one must/ought to” and گریست is the infinitive/impersonal verb “to weep/lament.” The clause means roughly “from which (i.e. on account of which) our pain must be lamented,” linking back to the preceding relative idea.
segments.s406-g95: بدو = به + او, “to him” (indirect object). گفت here is the reporting verb “said” (the provided lemma senses refer to ‘conversation’ rather than the verb form; the segment functions as ‘Jamasp said to him’). جاماسپ is the speaker’s name; کای شهریار is a vocative: کای = “O” and شهریار = “sovereign/king.”
segments.s406-g96: تواین = تو + این contracted as a demonstrative phrase “this” (i.e. ‘this’ day), placed before روز (day). را is the object marker. خوار = “despised/contemptible.” مایه = “cause/source” (here part of the idiom ‘مایه شدن’ = ‘become the cause of’). مدار is an imperative (‘do not make/regard’) here negating the action; the phrase therefore means “do not make/regard this day as a cause of contempt” → “do not hold this day in contempt.”
segments.s406-g9: چنین is an adverb meaning 'thus/so' placed sentence-initial for emphasis. گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (3rd person), with its subject omitted (understood from context). با + [مادر اسفندیار] is an indirect-object phrase: classical Persian often uses با where modern Persian would use به, so با مادر اسفندیار = 'to/with Esfandiyar's mother.'
segments.s406-g10: که introduces a reported-clause ('that'). با من = 'towards me' / 'to me'. همی is the archaic/perhaps emphatic pre-verbal particle (older form related to modern می-) marking continuous or habitual aspect. بد کند is a light verb construction: بد (bad) + کند (do) = 'treat badly' or 'do evil'; کند here is present/subjunctive-form used in reported speech. شهریار (the king) is postposed for emphasis (classical word order allows subject after verb).
segments.s406-g11: مرا گفت is 'he said to me' (مرا = me + object marker را; here the list has been split into م and را). چون functions here as a subordinating conjunction with conditional/temporal sense ('if/when' in older usage). کین لهراسپ شاه = 'vengeance against King Lohrasp' (کین = vengeance; لهراسپ شاه a name + title). Word order: indirect object 'مرا' before گفت, then the conditional clause introduced by چون.
segments.s406-g12: بخواهی is 2nd-person singular present/subjunctive of خواستن used in a conditional sense ('if you want/seek'). به مردی ز ارجاسپ شاه is a prepositional phrase: به + مردی = 'by/through a man'; ز (poetic form of از) = 'from/of'; ارجاسپ شاه = 'King Arjasp'. The clause as a whole reads 'if you want vengeance on Lohrasp, by/through a man of King Arjasp.'
segments.s406-g13: همان is a demonstrative/adjective 'that very / those same'. خواهران را is the direct object (خواهران = 'sisters', را = definite object marker). بیاری here is an archaic/poetic verb form (equivalent to بی‌آری / بیاوری) meaning 'bring/bring out/rescue' (second-person subjunctive/imperative sense 'you bring'). ز بند = 'from bondage' with ز = poetic از and بند = 'fetter, bond'.
segments.s406-g14: کنی is the (subjunctive/imperative) form of کردن used here as 'make' or 'do' (2nd/3rd person dependent on context). نام ما را = 'our name' with را marking the definite object. به گیتی بلند is a prepositional/adverbial phrase: به گیتی = 'in the world', بلند = 'high/lofty' (i.e. raise our fame). The order places the object before the verb in classical style.
segments.s406-g15: جهان is the subject 'the world'. از ... (بدان) is a classical/poetic 'from those' construction (ز/از + demonstrative/collective), here indicating removal 'from those (bad people)'. پاک کنی is 'you make clean/purge' (کنی = make/do), and بی‌خو appears as an adverbial or qualificative (archaic 'sleepless' or 'without rest') modifying the action—poetically implying relentless or thorough action. Word order is classical: subject, prepositional phrase, predicate.
segments.s406-g16: بکوشی is the present/subjunctive/imperative form of کوشیدن 'strive' (2nd person imperative/hortative sense 'strive!'). و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. آرایشی نو کنی = 'make a new آرایش' where آرایشی (from آرایش) = 'array/arrangement/adornment' and نو = 'new'; کنی again is the causative/do verb 'make' — together: 'strive and make a new arrangement.'
segments.s406-g49: پر از ... شد is a classical Persian phrase meaning “became full of …”. Here پر (followed by از) functions as a predicative adjective 'full (of)'. مادرش is the subject with the possessive suffix -ش ('his/her'), and شد is the past/past-resultive verb 'became'. The coordinating و links the two nouns that fill her (شرم and تشویر).
segments.s406-g50: ز گفته پشیمانی آمد برش is a short clause where ز گفته = 'from/at the words (that were said)' (ز used in poetic register), پشیمانی is the emotion (regret), آمد is the verb 'came' (i.e. 'came upon her'), and برش is the preposition بر + pronominal suffix -ش 'upon her' (written here as one token). The clause describes an experience coming over the mother because of what was said.
segments.s406-g51: This is an inverted, compressed narrative clause: بشد here is a past/eventive verb 'came/arrived' (classical usage), followed by پیش + name to mark place/presence 'before/in the presence of'. The two proper names follow, with the expected reading: 'Esfandiyar came into the presence of Gushtasp.'
segments.s406-g52: همی بود is a habitual/past progressive-styled copula 'he was (then)' (همی gives an ongoing or customary flavor). با introduces companions/accompaniment; رامش و میگسار are the companions. میگسار is a noun meaning 'cupbearer/wine-companion' used as a personal epithet here.
segments.s406-g53: دو روز و دو شب sets the duration 'two days and two nights'. باده خام خورد is a compact verbal phrase where باده 'wine' is the object, خام 'raw/unripe' qualifies the wine, and خورد is the past of خوردن 'consumed' (here 'drank'). The verb follows its object in this poetic order.
segments.s406-g54: بر ماهرویش دل آرام کرد is a causative/result clause: بر + ماهرویش ('for/over his moon‑faced one' — ماهرویش = ماهرو + possessive -ش) marks the beneficiary/reason, دل 'heart' is the object, آرام 'calm' is predicative/adjectival, and کرد 'made/did' is the light verb meaning 'caused to become calm' (i.e. 'he quieted his heart').
segments.s406-g55: سیم روز here (classical spelling of سیّم روز) is an adverbial time expression 'on the third day'. گشتاسپ is the proper-name subject, آگاه شد is the compound predicate 'became aware' (آگاه 'aware' + شد 'became'). The verb is past/resultive, giving the reportive tone.
segments.s406-g56: که introduces a subordinate clause 'that ...'. فرزند is 'the son/child' (subject of the subordinate clause). جوینده گاه شد is a verbal phrase: جوینده 'one who seeks' and شد 'became' — literally 'became a seeker'; گاه here is a time/occasional noun that can shade the sense to 'one who seeks at times' or simply is part of the idiomatic expression, but grammatically the clause reports the change of state.
segments.s406-g1: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' placed before بلبل to mark the source; شنیدم is the 1st-person past tense verb 'I heard' (standard SOV flexibility allows the prepositional phrase fronting); یکی داستان is an indefinite object phrase ('a certain tale' or 'a tale'), with یکی preceding the noun as in classical Persian.
segments.s406-g2: که introduces a relative clause modifying 'داستان' ('which'); برخواند is a 3rd-person verbal form (in Ferdowsi's style the -ad ending functions as a past/simple narrative) meaning 'he/it recited/read aloud'; از marks the source 'from' and گفته باستان is a noun phrase 'ancient saying/tale' giving the material recited.
segments.s406-g3: که begins this clause (continuing the narrative 'that...'); چون here functions as 'when'; مست is the predicative adjective 'drunk'; باز آمد is a compound where باز 'again' + آمد 'came' gives 'returned/came back'; اسفندیار is the proper name (subject) appearing after the verb, which is common in epic word order for emphasis.
segments.s406-g4: دژم گشته combines the adjective دژم 'sullen/gloomy' with گشته (the perfect participle/auxiliary form of گشتن) to mean 'had become sullen'; از خانه شهریار is a prepositional phrase 'from the house of the king', with شهریار (king) following the possessed noun خانه in normal genitive-like order.
segments.s406-g5: کتایون قیصر places the personal name and the title in apposition (classical style sometimes omits the ezafe); که opens a subordinate/relative clause referring back to her; بد modifies مادرش meaning 'ill' / 'badly' and ش is the pronominal clitic 'her', so 'بد مادرش' = 'her mother (was) ill'.
segments.s406-g6: گرفته is a past participle meaning 'seized' / 'taken' and here functions as 'was seized/afflicted'; شب و روز is the conventional adverbial phrase 'night and day'; اندر is a locative/prepositional particle 'in/within'; برش is composition of the preposition بر 'on/upon' plus the clitic ش 'her', yielding 'upon her' (i.e. afflicted her continuously).
segments.s406-g7: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'; از خواب 'from sleep' is the source phrase; بیدار شد is the lexical past expression 'awoke' (بیدار 'awake' + شد 'became'); تیره شب 'dark night' is a temporal modifier placed after the verb for poetic emphasis.
segments.s406-g8: یکی جام is an indefinite object 'a cup' (یکی = 'a certain' / 'a'); می‌خواست is the 3rd-person past/imperf. form 'he wanted/was wanting' using the progressive-prefixed می- common in narrative; و links the two actions; بگشاد is an archaic/past form of 'to open' (ب- prefix + گشاد/گشود) meaning 'he opened', and لب 'lip' is the object, understood as 'his lips' by context.
segments.s406-g25: که introduces the subordinate clause ('that' / 'that so'); بی‌کام is an adjective 'unfulfilled' describing او. The core predicate is تاج بر سر نهم, where تاج (crown) is the direct object placed with the prepositional phrase بر سر ('upon the head') and نهم is the 1st-person singular present/subjunctive form of کردن (here 'I place / I will place'), expressed as a suffix on the verb (understood from context). Word order is typical Persian: clause marker → adjectival phrase → person → object → location phrase → verb.
segments.s406-g26: همه کشور ایرانیان را is the object phrase following the previous verb; همه qualifies کشور as 'the whole/entire realm', ایرانیان is the genitive/attributive 'of the Iranians', and را is the definite object marker marking this noun phrase as the object to be given. دهم is the 1st-person singular present/subjunctive of کردن ('I give / I will give'), completing the verb sequence begun in the prior segment.
segments.s406-g27: ترا is the old/poetic accusative/benefactive 'to you' (here segmented as ت + را); بانوی شهر ایران is a noun phrase 'the lady/mistress of the city of Iran' (بانوی + modifier شهر ایران). کنم is the 1st-person singular present/subjunctive of کردن meaning 'I make / I shall make' — a promise or intention. Note the unusual poetic placement of the pronoun before the noun phrase.
segments.s406-g28: به زور is an adverbial phrase 'by force' (به + زور with به used adverbially). و links the two adverbials; به دل here likewise marks an adverbial 'in/with (the) heart' or 'by courage'. جنگ شیران is a compound meaning 'battle of the lions' i.e. 'lion‑hearted fighters' (shifting from literal to metaphorical). کن م is the repeated 1st-person verb 'I do / I shall do', showing the speaker's resolve to accomplish the prior making by these means.
segments.s406-g29: غمی شد is an existential/experiential construction literally 'a grief became' = 'grief arose'. ز گفتار او uses the poetic preposition ز (from/because of) plus گفتار او ('from his speech' / 'because of what he said'). مادرش is 'his mother' (مادر + possessive suffix ش), and it is the experiencer affected by that grief; the verb شد follows the noun (typical Persian ordering for 'X became Y').
segments.s406-g30: This clause describes the effect: همه پرنیان خار شد — literally 'all the silken robes became thorns'. همه qualifies پرنیان ('all the silken garments'); خار شد is 'became thorns'. بر + برش functions as a locative/relational phrase 'upon her' (literary phrasing). The whole line is a poetic image: the mother's fine garments feel like thorns because of her sorrow.
segments.s406-g31: بدانست is simple past 'he knew' (بد + دانست); کان introduces the reported content (classical/poetic complementizer functioning like 'that'), followed by a coordinated list of direct objects تاج و تخت و کلاه ('the crown and the throne and the cap'). Word order places the reporting verb before the content clause, a common classical construction.
segments.s406-g32: نبخشد is the negative form (ن- + بخشد) meaning 'would not bestow / will not give' in this reported context. ورا is the old-style indirect-object pronoun 'to him' (wara). نامبردار شاه is the agent noun phrase 'the renowned (or aforementioned) king' (نامبردار qualifying شاه). The sequence expresses that the king would not grant those things to him.
segments.s406-g41: This is an exclamative comparative phrase: چه نیکوتر از ... = “how much nobler than ...”. چه here is the exclamatory 'how' that introduces the praise; نیکوتر is the comparative of نیکو (“good/beautiful”) and از marks the standard of comparison. نره شیر is the noun phrase 'male lion' (نره = male, شیر = lion) and ژیان is an epithet attached to شیر (poetic/epithet form).
segments.s406-g42: This sequence gives the scene/location. به پیش پدر = 'before/in the presence of the father' (به + پیش = toward/in the presence of). بر کمر = 'upon (his) waist/back' (بر = on/upon, کمر = waist/back), and بر میان = 'at the middle/center' (بر = on; میان = middle). Word order places the locative phrases after the NP being described.
segments.s406-g43: چنین گفت با مادر اسفندیار = 'Thus he spoke to Esfandiyar's mother.' چنین is an adverb 'thus/so'; گفت is the past tense 'said/spoke' (here used with با + NP to mark the addressee); با introduces the person spoken to, and مادِر اسفندیار is a genitive phrase 'mother of Esfandiyar'.
segments.s406-g44: This is the reported utterance introduced by که: نیکو زد این داستان هوشیار = (roughly) 'Well uttered is this tale, O Hoshyār.' که here introduces the content; نیکو is 'well/good'; زد is the poet's idiomatic verb for 'uttered/struck/sounded' (used for reciting or telling); این داستان is the direct object 'this story'; هوشیار is a vocative/adjectival epithet 'O Wise One/alert one.'
segments.s406-g45: که پیش زنان راز هرگز مگوی = 'that (advice): before women, never tell a secret.' که introduces the statement; پیش زنان = 'in the presence of women' (پیش = in front of/in the presence of); راز is the direct object 'secret'; هرگز precedes the verb to give 'never'; مگوی is the negative imperative 'do not say' (archaic/poetic imperative form). Word order places the object and temporal/adverbial elements before the verb, typical in older poetic Persian.
segments.s406-g46: چو گویی سخن بازیابی به کوی = 'if/when you speak, the talk will be restored/spread to the quarter.' چو introduces a conditional ('if' / 'when'); گویی here refers to speaking (a verb/noun sense 'to say'/'speech'); سخن بازیابی is a verbal-noun phrase meaning the speech/talk will be repeated or come back (i.e., the rumor will be spread); به کوی specifies the destination/area ('to the quarter/neighborhood'). In sum: if you reveal it, the matter will be talked about around the quarter.
segments.s406-g47: مکن هیچ کاری به فرمان زن = 'Do not do any deed at a woman's command.' مکن is the negative imperative 'do not' (archaic), هیـچ کاری = 'any/any kind of action' (object), and به فرمان زن is a prepositional phrase 'at the command/orders of a woman' (به marks compliance with the command). The order is negative imperative + object + prepositional phrase.
segments.s406-g48: که هرگز نبینی زنی رای زن = 'for you will never see a woman [with] the counsel/opinion of a woman.' که introduces the explanatory clause; هرگز 'never' modifies the verb; نبینی is a negative present/subjunctive form 'you will not see / you do not see'; زنی is the indefinite/direct object 'a woman'; رای زن is a genitive phrase 'the counsel/opinion of a woman' (رای = counsel/opinion). The clause warns that one will not observe women acting by (or holding to) such counsel.
segments.s406-g33: بدو گفت = 'he said to him' with the prepositional pronoun بدو ('to him') placed before the verb گفت (past 'said') — a common poetic ordering. کای is the vocative particle 'O', followed by the adjectival past participle رنج‌دیده 'distressed/afflicted' modifying the noun پسر 'son'. The whole line is direct address: 'He said to him, O distressed son...'.
segments.s406-g34: ز گیتی 'from the world' (ز = from; گیتی = world). چه جوید is an interrogative clause: چه = 'what'; جوید is 3rd‑person singular present/subjunctive of جستن/جُویدن meaning 'seeks/asks for' (in poetry present/subjunctive can express general or future). دل is the subject 'heart' placed after the verb (poetic word order). تاجور functions as an epithet modifying دل ('a heart that seeks the crown'), but the provided lexical senses for تاجور do not match this poetic usage.
segments.s406-g35: مگر here introduces a conditional/exclusion 'unless' that sets up the condition; it precedes a coordinated list: گنج و فرمان و رای و سپاه ('treasure and command and counsel and army'). The conjunction و simply links the items in the list; no finite verb appears in this segment.
segments.s406-g36: تو داری = 'you have' (تو subject + داری present tense 'possess'). برین is the poetic/indexical 'this' formed from بر + ین (here treat ین as the orthographic/old form of این), so 'برین بر' = 'over/above this'; فزونی = 'increase, excess'. مخواه is the negative imperative/subjunctive (مَ + خواه) meaning 'do not desire/ask for' — an admonition: 'do not wish for more.'
segments.s406-g37: یکی opens with 'one (thing)/in one respect' — a prefatory word. تاج دارد = 'has a crown' (تاج noun + دارد present of داشتن). پدر is the subject 'the father'; بر پسر is a prepositional phrase 'over/with respect to the son' (بر = on/over + پسر), indicating the relation in which the father holds the crown.
segments.s406-g38: تو داری 'you have' (present). دگر functions as a contrastive adverb 'on the other hand/otherwise'. A coordinated list follows: لشکر و بوم و بر — 'army, bōm (homeland), and bar' (بر here is used in the older/poetic sense 'domain/land'), completing the contrast with what the father possesses. The final بر is an archaic noun usage and does not match the simple prepositional senses in the provided list.
segments.s406-g39: چو او بگذرد = temporal clause 'when he passes/is gone' (چو = 'when'; او + بگذرد, present/subjunctive 'passes' — in classical poetry used for future or eventual action). تاج و تختش is the coordinated subject 'his crown and his throne' (تخت + pronominal suffix ش 'his'). تراست is a fused expression equivalent to 'ترا است' = 'it is for you/it belongs to you' (ترا = to you; است = is), a standard poetic way to state possession: 'will be yours.'
segments.s406-g40: A second coordinated list ('بزرگی و شاهی و بختش')—'greatness, kingship, and his fortune'—followed again by تراست 'is yours.' Note بختش includes the genitive suffix ش 'his'. The repetition of تراست emphasizes that these qualities too will pass to you.
segments.s406-g97: ورا is a third-person prepositional/pronominal form used here as a possessive/indirect pronoun ('for him' → 'his') placed before the noun (classical order). هوش is the noun 'intellect / wit.' در زاولستان is a locative prepositional phrase 'in Zābulistān.' بود is the past copula 'was,' so the whole segment states possession/location in the past.
segments.s406-g98: به دست is an instrumental/possessive phrase 'in the hand / in the power (of).' Here به functions as 'by/in the hand of' and دست carries the sense of 'power/possession.' تهم‌پور دستان is a personal name built of elements; پور acts as the name‑forming element '‑pur' (not its literal 'son' meaning). بود is again the past copula. The segment therefore says the wit was held by (in the power of) that person.
segments.s406-g99: This is an inverted classical word order: به جاماسپ گفت = 'said to Jamasp' (به + name = indirect object before the verb). آنگهی is the temporal adverb 'then / thereupon.' شهریار is the subject 'the king,' placed after the verb for emphatic or narrative order ('then the king said to Jamasp').
segments.s406-g100: به من is the indirect object 'to me' or 'upon me.' بر is a directional/prefixive element here ('upon' / 'against') that combines with the verb بگردد; بگردد is a non‑past/subjunctive/optative form of گردیدن meaning 'turn / befall / happen to' and here functions as 'will (it) befall.' بد روزگار = 'bad fortune / ill days' (بد modifies روزگار). The clause is a rhetorical question asking whether ill fate will fall upon me.
segments.s406-g101: که‌گر (conditional 'if') introduces a protasis. من is the first‑person subject. سر تاج شاهنشهی is a noun phrase: سر 'head' used in a compound with تاج 'crown' and شاهنشهی 'imperial' (postpositive adjective/attributive), together meaning 'the imperial crown' or 'the crown of the empire.' The phrase is the object of the following verb.
segments.s406-g102: سپارم is the first‑person singular subjunctive/optative of سپردن 'to entrust / to give over' ('that I should entrust / if I were to entrust'). بدو is the clitic/phrase 'to him' (به + او). تاج و تخت مهی is the coordinated object: تاج 'crown' and تخت 'throne' with the epithet مهی 'moon‑like' (a poetic adjective placed after the noun). The clause completes the conditional: 'if I entrust the imperial crown, to him I would entrust crown and moonlike throne.'
segments.s406-g103: نبیند is the negative form of دیدن in classical narrative—here a third‑person negative (rendered in English as 'is not seen' or 'did not see/see him'); contextually it functions as 'is not seen' on that soil. بر و بوم is a fixed collocation 'land and dwelling / soil' with بر as a locative preposition 'on/upon' followed by بوم 'land.' زاولستان is the place name 'Zābulistān.'
segments.s406-g104: نداند is the negative present/past sense of دانستن 'knows not' (here 'no one knows'). کس with a negative verb takes the sense 'no one' rather than 'someone.' او را is the third‑person object with the definite object marker را. به کاولستان is a locative phrase 'in/at Kābulistān.' Word order (verb before کس) is emphatic/archaic: 'no one knows him in Kābulistān.'
segments.s406-g105: This is a short interrogative clause with the verb placed first for poetic emphasis. شود is the classical/subjunctive/optative third‑person form meaning “may/shall be” or “will be” (here rendered as “will he be”); ایمن is a predicative adjective “safe/protected”; از introduces the source/agent “from”; گردش روزگار is a genitive phrase “the turning/circuit of fate/time.” The verb-first order and question mark make the whole a rhetorical question.
segments.s406-g106: بود here is a past/is form (classical simple past or stative) used to ask whether something was the case — “was.” اختر is the noun “star”; the suffix -ش on نیکش is the possessive pronoun “his” attached to نیک (here meaning “auspicious/good”), so نیکش = “his auspicious one/auspicious star.” آموزگار is a predicate noun “teacher/guide.” The order places the copula before predicate for rhythmic effect: “Was his auspicious star his guide?”
segments.s406-g107: چنین is an adverb “thus/so.” داد is the past tense verb “gave” (here forming the verbal phrase “gave an answer”); پاسخ is the direct object “answer/reply.” ستاره شمر is the nominative agent “the star Shamr” (the proper name follows ستاره). Word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect: adverb + verb + object + subject.
segments.s406-g108: که introduces a subordinate clause (“that/which”). بر چرخ گردان is a prepositional phrase: بر = “on/upon,” چرخ گردان = “the revolving wheel” (a common image for the turning heavens/fate). نیابد is the negative present 3rd‑person form of یافتن “to find” (here “does not find”); گذر is the noun “passage” (object). Poetic inversion places the verb before its object: “does not find passage.”
segments.s406-g109: ازین is the compound prepositional/ demonstrative “from this” (از + این). بر شده is a verbal/periphrastic construction: بر (upon/against) + شده (past participle/has become) meaning “was turned upon” or “fell upon.” تیز چنگ اژدها is a noun phrase where تیز (sharp) qualifies چنگ (claw/talon) and اژدها is in genitive attachment “of the dragon.” The whole segment describes an action resulting from the previous clause: “from this was turned the dragon’s sharp talon.”
segments.s406-g110: به marks the target/recipient of the action (“upon/to”). مردی و دانش joins two nouns — “a man and [of] learning” — functioning idiomatically as “a man of courage/virility and learning” (an appositive or epithet). که introduces a relative clause modifying that man: آمد is the simple past “came,” and رها is an adjective/participial predicate “free/unbound/unharmed,” placed after the verb (poetic inversion). Together: “upon a man of courage and learning, who came away free.”
segments.s406-g111: بباشد is an optative/subjunctive form meaning “may it be” or “let it be” (classical wish or general assertion). همه بودنی is a nominalized phrase: همه “all” + بودنی “that which is / beings / things that exist.” بی‌گمان is an adverb “undoubtedly / without doubt.” The clause is a general, emphatic statement/wish: “May all that exists be without doubt” or “Let all existence be certain.”
segments.s406-g112: نجستست is an archaic/poetic negative/resultative form meaning essentially “did not escape / was not spared” (here describing failure to avoid something). ازو is the prepositional phrase “from him/it.” مرد دانا زمان is a nominal chain: مرد “man” qualified by دانا “wise/learned” and further specified by زمان “of the time/age” — “the wise man of the time.” The clause says that this wise man did not escape from him/it (i.e., was not spared).
segments.s406-g17: همه modifies the following nouns as a collective determiner ('the whole/all'); the phrase پادشاهی و لشکر is the compound nominal subject/thing being possessed. تراست is a poetic copular/possessive construction meaning 'is yours' (تـ + راست), placed after the possessed items—classical Persian often puts the possessee before the verb 'is'.
segments.s406-g18: همان is a demonstrative/emphatic ('that very' / 'the same') qualifying گنج. با introduces accompaniment ('with throne and helmet'), and تراست repeats the possessive copula 'is yours'. Word order is noun + with-phrase + predicate, typical of Ferdowsi's poetic inversion.
segments.s406-g19: کنون is a temporal adverb ('now'). چون here functions as a subordinating conjunction 'when' (classical/poetic usage). برآرد is a 3rd-person verb 'raises/brings forth' (present/habitual narrative form). سپهر آفتاب places the poetic subject and object together ('the heavens' + 'the sun'), with سپهر governing the action.
segments.s406-g20: سر شاه is a poetic way to refer to the king (literally 'the king's head' → 'the king'). بیدار is the predicative adjective 'awake'; گردد (from گشت/گرد) is a 3rd-person present/subjunctive 'becomes' used as the main verb. ز خواب is an ablative phrase 'from sleep'. The verb follows the predicate in this classical order.
segments.s406-g21: بگویم is a 1st-person verbal form ('I will say / I tell'); پدر را uses را after 'father' to mark the definite person involved (classical Persian can use را with the addressee or definite object). سخنها is the direct content 'the words/tales', and که گفت is a relative clause 'which he said' modifying سخنها. Word order places the indirect/definite person before the content in this poetic register.
segments.s406-g22: ندارد is a negative present 'does not have / does not (do)'; here it negates the action of نهفت, so the whole means 'he does not hide'. ز من is the ablative 'from me' and راستیها (plural) = 'truths' or 'truthfulness'. نهفت (infinitive/past-stem form of نهفتن) functions as the verb 'to hide/conceal' so together the clause reads 'he does not hide truths from me.'
segments.s406-g23: وگر is the conditional 'and if' (poetic). هیچ here functions as an indefinite ('any' / 'any at all') modifying تاب 'brightness, radiance'. اندر آرد is a compound verb 'brings into/causes to enter' (اندر + آرد), and به چهر is a prepositional phrase 'into the face/appearance' (چهر = visage/appearance). Together: 'and if any radiance comes into his face.'
segments.s406-g24: به یزدان is an oath-like prepositional phrase 'by God' or 'to the Divine'. که introduces a relative clause describing that deity. بر پای دارد سپهر is an inverted poetic order: بر پای ('on the base/footing') + دارد ('holds') + سپهر ('the heavens'), literally 'who holds the heavens on their base.' The verb دارد governs سپهر, but word order is shifted for metrical/poetic effect.

sentences.s406-s33: Will he set the imperial crown upon his head? Does he possess a firm footing, good and moon‑like?
sentences.s406-s34: When the sage of Iran heard the words, he fixed his gaze on those ancient zij (astronomical tables).
sentences.s406-s35: From his knowledge he filled the brows with radiance; from anxious care he filled the eyelashes with water (tears).
sentences.s406-s36: He kept saying, 'Alas, a bad day and an ill star!' Fire continually rained down upon my head.
sentences.s406-s37: Would that fate had hurled me before Farrokh Zarrir into the lion's claws.
sentences.s406-s38: And if my father himself had not slain me, Jamasp would not have received me — ill-fated.
sentences.s406-s39: Nor did you even see him in the inner dust, thrown there like a feather, soaked in blood.
sentences.s406-s40: Like Esfandiyār, who, from his claw, ripped the lion's heart because of his intent.
sentences.s406-s57: From that the king's heart became pensive, and sorrow and pain likewise became his companions.
sentences.s406-s58: Evil thoughts and the turning of fortune were always his teachers in wrongdoing.
sentences.s406-s41: He swept the world's enemies clean; in battle there was no match for him.
sentences.s406-s42: He freed the world from ill‑wishers and made it fearless; he cleft the dragon's body in two.
sentences.s406-s43: From now on his sorrow must be borne; much tumult and bitterness must be tasted.
sentences.s406-s44: To him the king said, 'O chosen man, speak, and do not depart from the path of knowledge.'
sentences.s406-s29: He kept brooding in his heart; continually a longing for crown and throne came upon him.
sentences.s406-s30: At that time the king summoned Jamasp and those same diviners of Lohrasp.
sentences.s406-s31: They went aside with their astronomical tables; the king asked about Esfandiyar.
sentences.s406-s32: Would he have a long life? Would he sit in joy and tranquillity and ease?
sentences.s406-s45: Come now — hurry and speak with me, for this question has brought bitterness to my face.
sentences.s406-s46: If he were a spahbed like Zarir, it would be ill for me to live after this.
sentences.s406-s47: Who in the world has wit at hand — from which our pain must be bewailed?
sentences.s406-s48: Jamasp said to him, 'O king, do not regard this day as contemptible.'
sentences.s406-s5: Thus he said to Esfandiyar's mother that the king is treating me badly.
sentences.s406-s6: He told me that if you seek vengeance on King Lohrasp, (seek it) by a man of King Arjasp.
sentences.s406-s7: Free those same sisters from their bonds, and make our name lofty in the world.
sentences.s406-s8: Purge the world of those wicked ones; strive and fashion a new arrangement.
sentences.s406-s25: His mother was filled with shame and consternation; remorse at what had been said came over her.
sentences.s406-s26: Esfandiyar went before Gushtasp; he was with Ramash and the cupbearer.
sentences.s406-s27: For two days and two nights he drank raw wine; he quieted his heart over his moon‑faced beloved.
sentences.s406-s28: On the third day Gushtasp became aware that his son had taken to seeking (was become a seeker).
sentences.s406-s1: I heard a tale from the nightingale that it recited from ancient lore.
sentences.s406-s2: When Esfandiyar returned drunk, he had become sullen after coming from the king's house.
sentences.s406-s3: Katayun the Caesar, whose mother had taken ill, was afflicted night and day.
sentences.s406-s4: When he awoke from sleep on a dark night, he wanted a cup and opened his lips.
sentences.s406-s13: That, even if he remains unfulfilled, I will set the crown upon his head and give him the whole realm of the Iranians.
sentences.s406-s14: I will make you the lady of the city of Iran; I will do it by force and by rousing lion‑hearted fighters to battle.
sentences.s406-s15: A sorrow came upon his mother from his words; all her silken robes turned into thorns upon her.
sentences.s406-s16: He knew that the crown, the throne, and the cap would not be bestowed upon him by the renowned king.
sentences.s406-s21: How much nobler is the young male lion, seated before his father upon the father's waist in the middle.
sentences.s406-s22: Thus he said to Esfandiyar's mother: “Well said — this tale, O Wise One.”
sentences.s406-s23: That: never tell a secret in the presence of women; if you do, the talk will spread through the quarter.
sentences.s406-s24: Do nothing at a woman's command, for you will never find a woman following a woman's counsel.
sentences.s406-s17: He said to him, 'O distressed son, what does a crown‑seeking heart want from the world?'
sentences.s406-s18: Unless you have treasure, command, counsel, and an army, do not seek increase beyond this.
sentences.s406-s19: In one respect the father has the crown over the son; you, on the other hand, have the army, homeland, and realm.
sentences.s406-s20: When he is gone, his crown and his throne will be yours; his greatness, kingship, and fortune will be yours as well.
sentences.s406-s49: His wits were in Zābulistān, and they were in the hand of Tahm-pūr Dastān.
sentences.s406-s50: Then the king said to Jamasp: 'Will ill fortune turn upon me?'
sentences.s406-s51: If I were to hand over the imperial crown, to him I would give the crown and the moonlike throne.
sentences.s406-s52: He is not (or was not) seen on the land and soil of Zābulistān; no one knows him in Kābulistān.
sentences.s406-s53: Will he be safe from the turns of fate? Was his auspicious star his guide?
sentences.s406-s54: Thus the star Shamr answered: that on the revolving wheel there is no passage.
sentences.s406-s55: From that the dragon's sharp talon was turned upon a man of courage and learning who came away free.
sentences.s406-s56: May all that exists be without doubt; the wise man of the time did not escape from him.
sentences.s406-s9: All the kingdom and the army are yours; that same treasure, with throne and helmet, is yours.
sentences.s406-s10: Now, when the heavens lift up the sun, the king awakens from sleep.
sentences.s406-s11: I will tell my father the words that he spoke; he does not hide truths from me.
sentences.s406-s12: And if any brightness should enter into his visage, by God who supports the heavens on their base,
